synthesis_text: Implement rate limiting or login throttling per IP/user (e.g., limit attempts, introduce exponential back‑off or temporary lockout). This directly curbs brute‑force traffic, reduces the number of failed‑login DB writes, and therefore alleviates lock contention and latency while improving security.

Implement application‑level rate limiting or login throttling per IP/user (e.g., limit attempts, temporary lockout) to block brute‑force attacks. This immediately cuts the volume of failed‑login DB writes, reduces hot‑spot contention and lock wait times, and improves both stability and security.

First, add application‑level rate limiting or login throttling per IP and per user (e.g., limit the number of login attempts in a short window, apply exponential back‑off or temporary lockout). This directly cuts the flood of failed attempts, reducing the number of DB writes and reads that cause lock contention, while also mitigating brute‑force attacks. After rate limiting is in place, address the remaining DB contention by reviewing indexes and keeping login transactions short.
